statement.markdown: You are given a binary tree where each node has an integer value. Your task is to calculate and return the height of this binary tree. The height of a binary tree is the number of edges between the root and the deepest leaf node. If the tree is empty, return 0.
statement.inputFormat: The binary tree is represented as a list of nodes, where each node is an object with properties 'value' and 'left' and 'right', representing the node's value and left and right child nodes, respectively.
statement.outputFormat: Return the height of the binary tree.
statement.notes: The height of a tree with only one node is 0. The height of a tree with two nodes is 1. The height of a tree with three nodes is 2, etc.

examples.explanation: Height of the binary tree is 3
Height of the binary tree is 3

hints.text: Use depth-first search (DFS) to traverse the binary tree

editorial.title: Calculating the Height of a Binary Tree
editorial.intuition: The height of a binary tree is the maximum number of edges between the root node and a leaf node
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach: perform a level-order traversal of the tree and count the number of levels
editorial.betterSolution: Use depth-first search to traverse the tree and calculate the height
editorial.optimalSolution: Use a recursive DFS function to traverse the tree and calculate the height
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The height of a binary tree is the maximum number of edges between the root node and a leaf node
editorial.dryRun: Traverse the binary tree using DFS and calculate the height
commonMistakes: forgetting to handle the case where the tree is empty
